title: Handling Morphology & Typos
concept: Subword tokenizers decompose 'unhappiness' to ['un', 'happi', 'ness'] and typos like 'happines' to ['happi', 'nes'], letting models process them without crashes.

exercises.prompt: How do modern AI models handle complex words and spelling mistakes without crashing?

options.text: By translating everything into emoji format first.
By breaking words down into smaller parts like prefixes and roots.
By only reading the first and last letters of every word.

exercises.explain: Words are made of prefixes, suffixes, and roots (morphology). Subword tokenizers leverage this to split words into smaller pieces.

cards.title: Handling the Unexpected
cards.body: What happens when an AI model encounters a word it has never seen before, or a word with a typo like 'computr'? In the old days, the AI would just give up and label it as 'unknown'. Today, we use a smart trick to break words down into tiny, recognizable pieces so the AI never gets lost.

cards.title: What is Subword Tokenization?
cards.body: Subword tokenizers solve the 'unknown word' problem by breaking words into smaller units called subwords. For example, the complex word 'unhappiness' is broken into ['un', 'happi', 'ness']. If a user makes a typo like 'happines', the tokenizer splits it into ['happi', 'nes'], allowing the AI to still understand the root meaning instead of throwing an error.

cards.title: The LEGO Analogy
cards.body: Imagine playing with LEGO blocks. Instead of molding a single, custom plastic piece for every shape in the universe, you use small, standardized bricks. By snapping standard blocks together, you can build a spaceship, a castle, or even a brand-new toy. Subword tokenization is exactly like this: it breaks complex or misspelled words down into standard, reusable word-parts.

cards.title: Subwords in Action
cards.body:
Let's look at how a subword tokenizer processes words:

• Input: "unhappiness"
• Output tokens: ['un', 'happi', 'ness']

• Input with typo: "happines"
• Output tokens: ['happi', 'nes']

Even though 'happines' is misspelled, the model recognizes the root 'happi' and can guess the user's intent!

cards.title: The Dictionary Myth
cards.body: People often think that if a word has a typo, the AI must have a dictionary containing every possible typo to understand it. In reality, subword tokenizers don't need a list of every typo. By breaking the typo down into smaller pieces, they can easily make sense of the recognizable parts.

cards.title: Word vs. Subword Comparison
cards.body: See how subword tokenization keeps the core meaning intact compared to old-school word tokenization when handling typos.

exercises.prompt: How does a subword tokenizer handle a complex word like 'unhappiness'?

options.text: It splits the word into smaller, meaningful pieces.
It replaces the word with a random number.
It alerts the programmer to manually fix the spelling.

exercises.explain: Subword tokenizers split words into smaller parts (like ['un', 'happi', 'ness']), allowing the model to handle root meanings and grammatical variations without needing to know every exact word in advance.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a user makes a typo, a subword tokenizer will always fail and mark the word as completely unknown.
exercises.explain: Subword tokenizers are highly effective at handling typos because they can isolate the misspelled part and still read the rest of the word correctly.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the explanation of tokenization.
exercises.sentence: When a model encounters a typo, it breaks the word down into smaller ______ so it can still understand the root meaning.
exercises.explain: By breaking words down into smaller subwords, the tokenizer ensures that the model can process unfamiliar variations and spelling mistakes instead of failing.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following demonstrates how a subword tokenizer handles a typo?
exercises.explain: Option A shows a subword tokenizer breaking a typo down into recognizable components. Option B is character tokenization, and Option C is word tokenization (which fails on typos by using UNK).

exercises.prompt: You are designing a chatbot and want to make sure it doesn't crash or get confused when users make typos (like typing 'runing' instead of 'running') or use prefixes. How does subword tokenization solve this problem?

options.text: It marks the entire misspelled word as an UNK (unknown) token.
It automatically corrects the typo in the text before processing it.
It breaks them into smaller, known subwords like ['run', 'ing'] or ['happi', 'nes'].
It deletes the misspelled parts of the word to save computing memory.

exercises.explain: Subword tokenization breaks down unseen, complex, or misspelled words into recognizable pieces. This allows the model to process words like 'unhelpfulness' and typos like 'runing' without running into frustrating 'unknown' errors.
